summary: Simon introduces a blog series on Data Warehouse Automation (DWA), arguing that while the fundamental DWH architecture hasn't changed much since IBM created it in 1986, the approach to building data warehouses must evolve. He contends that traditional manual approaches are too slow for modern business needs, and DWA tools represent the future of the discipline. The post serves as a table of contents linking to deeper explorations of why DWA isn't more popular, its benefits, and available tools.
key_points: The core data warehouse architecture has remained remarkably stable since IBM's 1986 design
Traditional manual DWH development is too slow for modern business requirements
DWA tools will revitalize data warehousing's reputation despite competition from data lakes and big data
Data warehouses aren't going away despite the hype around newer technologies
money_quotes: Very long time ago, 1986, 31 years ago to be precise, IBM in Europe created the very first architecture of a data warehouse. And it seems to be a masterpiece as it didn't change much since.
No matter how we want to improve the architecture, it has to be automated as much as possible.
Nowadays it became too slow for serving the business needs doing it the traditional way.
I strongly believe that DWA tools are the future and will boost the Data Warehousing reputation back to earlier years.
I don't think DWH will go away anytime soon.
key_insight: Data warehouse automation is essential because traditional manual development can no longer keep pace with modern business demands, and DWA tools will restore the relevance of data warehousing in an era dominated by big data hype.
